title: Is the zero-divisor graph Γ(Z_5 × Z_25) prime?
question: Does the zero-divisor graph of the ring Z_5 × Z_25 admit a prime labeling?
canonical_source: Fox and Mooney, "On prime labelings of zero-divisor graphs," Congressus Numerantium 236 (2025), Conjecture 4.4, https://doi.org/10.61091/cn236-06
canonical_statement: Conjecture 4.4. The zero-divisor graph Γ(Z_p × Z_{q^2}) is prime for all prime numbers p and q.
intended_statement: The zero-divisor graph Γ(Z_5 × Z_25) is prime.
definitions: Z_n denotes the ring of integers modulo n.
For a commutative ring R, Γ(R) has as vertices the nonzero zero-divisors of R, with x adjacent to y iff xy = 0.
In Z_25, the nonzero zero-divisors are exactly the nonzero multiples of 5.
A prime labeling of an N-vertex graph is a bijection to {1, ..., N} such that adjacent vertices receive coprime labels.
why_reasoning_friendly: This keeps the same two-factor structure as Z_3 × Z_25 while staying small at 44 vertices. The symmetry between the 5-primary factors may make an explicit labeling pattern easier to guess than in more uneven parameter choices.
why_low_token: Only 44 vertices are present, and the ring has a short description. A candidate witness or obstruction remains concise and checker-friendly.
verifier_hint: Enumerate the 44 nonzero zero-divisors of Z_5 × Z_25, generate the adjacency relation by multiplication-to-zero, and verify Nat.gcd = 1 on each edge.
lean_hint: Model the graph from ZMod 5 × ZMod 25 or a finite partition of vertex classes and verify a bijection to Fin 44 is coprime on edges.
red_flags: This is not the first omitted case in Conjecture 4.4, so rediscovery risk is somewhat higher than for Z_3 × Z_25.
Because both factors are 5-primary, there may be a hidden simplification or obstruction not obvious from the family statement alone.
rediscovery_risk: Moderate: this is a second-step instance in Conjecture 4.4 rather than the smallest omitted one, but exact and alternate searches still did not expose any later instance-specific theorem, example, or discussion.
why_still_appears_open: The exact-instance search produced no relevant hit, the alternate ASCII search surfaced only generic zero-divisor-graph results, and the canonical source still jumps from Theorems 2.12, 2.14, and 2.15 to Conjecture 4.4 without resolving this p = 5, q = 5 case. The shared 2025-2026 DOI/source search showed only the source paper and archive pages.

title: Is the zero-divisor graph Γ(Z_2^8) prime?
question: Does the zero-divisor graph of the 8-fold direct product ring Z_2^8 admit a prime labeling?
canonical_source: Fox and Mooney, "On prime labelings of zero-divisor graphs," Congressus Numerantium 236 (2025), Conjecture 4.2, https://doi.org/10.61091/cn236-06
canonical_statement: Conjecture 4.2. The zero-divisor graph Γ(Z_2^n) is prime for any positive integer n.
intended_statement: The zero-divisor graph Γ(Z_2^8) is prime.
definitions: Z_2^n means the direct product of n copies of the ring Z_2.
Vertices of Γ(R) are the nonzero zero-divisors of R; x and y are adjacent iff xy = 0.
For R = Z_2^n, vertices can be identified with nonempty proper subsets of {1, ..., n}, with adjacency corresponding to disjoint supports.
A prime labeling of an N-vertex graph is a bijection to {1, ..., N} such that adjacent vertices receive coprime labels.
why_reasoning_friendly: The graph has the cleanest model in the queue: nonempty proper subsets of an 8-set with adjacency = disjointness. The paper explicitly says the bit-pattern labeling idea works for n = 6 and 7 and that only a general proof is missing, so n = 8 is the first concrete frontier step.
why_low_token: The witness space is large at 254 vertices, but the structure compresses to 8-bit subsets and any successful argument is likely to be a rule-based construction rather than a raw table.
verifier_hint: Represent vertices as nonempty proper subsets of {1, ..., 8}, test disjointness for edges, and check gcd(label(A), label(B)) = 1 on every adjacent pair.
lean_hint: Use nonempty proper Finsets of Fin 8 as vertices, adjacency as Disjoint, and formalize a bijection to Fin 254 satisfying Nat.Coprime on adjacent pairs.
red_flags: The graph is much larger than the other queue entries, so a brute-force search would be a mistake.
Exact-instance web search is noisy because the notation clashes with unrelated uses of Γ and Z_2^8.
rediscovery_risk: Moderate: the paper already gives labelings through n = 7 and states the general conjecture, so the intended n = 8 target is inferred as the first omitted case; exact and alternate searches did not reveal any later instance-specific resolution, but notation noise is higher here.
why_still_appears_open: Inside the canonical source, the authors state that the construction works for n = 6 and 7 but that a general proof still eludes them, and a direct find for Z_2^8 in the paper returns nothing. The exact-notation web search was swallowed by unrelated Γ/Zγ noise, the alternate search found other zero-divisor-graph papers but no solution to this instance, and the 2025-2026 DOI/source search still only surfaced the 2025 Fox-Mooney paper and archive pages.

title: Is the zero-divisor graph Γ(Z_5 × Z_5 × Z_2) prime?
question: Does the zero-divisor graph of the ring Z_5 × Z_5 × Z_2 admit a prime labeling?
canonical_source: Fox and Mooney, "On prime labelings of zero-divisor graphs," Congressus Numerantium 236 (2025), Conjecture 4.3, https://doi.org/10.61091/cn236-06
canonical_statement: Conjecture 4.3. The zero-divisor graph Γ(Z_p × Z_p × Z_q) is prime for all prime numbers p and q.
intended_statement: The zero-divisor graph Γ(Z_5 × Z_5 × Z_2) is prime.
definitions: Z_p denotes the ring of integers modulo the prime p.
For a commutative ring R, Γ(R) has as vertices the nonzero zero-divisors of R, with x adjacent to y iff xy = 0.
For R = Z_5 × Z_5 × Z_2, multiplication is coordinatewise, so adjacency is determined coordinatewise.
A prime labeling of an N-vertex graph is a bijection to {1, ..., N} such that adjacent vertices receive coprime labels.
why_reasoning_friendly: This is the first repeated-prime case left after Theorems 2.9 and 2.11 cover the p = 2 and p = 3 families. The graph has only 33 vertices and splits cleanly into coordinate-support types, so a short constructive labeling or a sharp obstruction is plausible.
why_low_token: The full graph has 33 vertices. A complete witness is a 33-label assignment, and the ring structure compresses to a small number of support classes.
verifier_hint: Enumerate the 33 nonzero zero-divisors of Z_5 × Z_5 × Z_2, build edges by coordinatewise zero product, and check gcd(label(x), label(y)) = 1 on every edge.
lean_hint: Model vertices as the nonzero zero-divisors of ZMod 5 × ZMod 5 × ZMod 2 or via explicit support classes, then formalize a bijection to Fin 33 satisfying Nat.Coprime on adjacent pairs.
red_flags: The paper states a family conjecture, not this concrete instance by name.
A naive search over all labelings is unnecessary and should stay off the critical path.
rediscovery_risk: Low to moderate: the exact instance is inferred from Conjecture 4.3 after the source proves only the p = 2 and p = 3 repeated-prime families; exact-notation and alternate-notation searches did not surface any later instance-specific resolution.
why_still_appears_open: The exact-instance search did not return any matching resolution, the alternate ASCII search returned only generic or irrelevant zero-divisor-graph material, and the canonical source still lists Conjecture 4.3 as open while Theorem 2.11 only settles Γ(Z_3 × Z_3 × Z_p). A 2025-2026 DOI/source search surfaced the Fox-Mooney paper and its volume listing but no sequel resolving this case.
